title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption:
Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant

The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, Acropora, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching.

This line chart tracks the percentage of Caribbean reefs where the coral genus Acropora was either present or dominant from 1950 to 2007. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis marks years in ten-year increments. Two distinct lines represent the data: an orange line for reefs where Acropora was present and a blue line for reefs where it was dominant. In 1950, the share of reefs with Acropora present began at approximately 90%, while the share where it was dominant started near 80%. Both metrics show a general downward trend over the observed period. The orange line dips to roughly 65% by 1965 before rising slightly to about 75% in the mid-1970s, then declines steadily to just under 20% by 2007. The blue line drops sharply to 50% by 1955 and fluctuates between 45% and 60% until the mid-1970s. After 1975, the dominant share falls consistently, reaching a low of roughly 5% around 2002 before ending near 8% in 2007. Throughout the timeline, the percentage of reefs where Acropora was present remains higher than the percentage where it was dominant. The visual data illustrates a significant reduction in the prevalence of this coral group across the region over the second half of the 20th century and into the early 21st century.
